# Kafka Record Batch Serialization - Javadoc Enhancement This document contains unified diffs for adding comprehensive Javadoc to Kafka's record batch serialization classes. ## Overview These three classes form the core of Kafka's message batch serialization in the v2 format: - **RecordBatch**: Interface defining the contract for a batch of records - **DefaultRecordBatch**: Concrete implementation for magic version 2 and above - **MemoryRecords**: In-memory collection of record batches with filtering and transformation capabilities --- ## 1. RecordBatch Interface Enhancement ### File: `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/RecordBatch.java` ```diff --- a/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/RecordBatch.java +++ b/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/RecordBatch.java @@ -24,10 +24,31 @@ import java.util.OptionalLong; import java.util.OptionalLong; /** - * A record batch is a container for records. In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1), - * a batch consisted always of a single record if no compression was enabled, but could contain - * many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records - * regardless of compression. + * A record batch is a container for records in the Kafka protocol. + * + * In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1), a batch consisted of either a single record + * (if no compression was enabled) or multiple records (if compression was used). Newer versions (magic + * versions 2 and above) generally contain many records regardless of compression strategy. + * + *

Role in Kafka Protocol

+ * RecordBatch is the fundamental unit of data organization in Kafka logs and network communication. Each + * batch contains metadata (offsets, timestamps, producer information, CRC checksum) and a sequence of + * individual records. The batch format supports idempotent producers, transactional writes, and compression. + * + *

Thread Safety

+ * Not thread-safe. Implementations of this interface are typically backed by + * {@link java.nio.ByteBuffer} which is not thread-safe. Concurrent access from multiple threads requires + * external synchronization. Most use cases involve reading batches from log segments or network channels + * in a single-threaded context, making synchronization unnecessary. + * + *

Performance Characteristics

+ *
    + *
  • Header access (baseOffset, magic, timestamp, etc.) is O(1) and reads directly from the underlying buffer
    • + *
  • Record iteration may require decompression of the entire batch if compression is enabled
    • + *
  • Use {@link #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)} for low-memory iteration of compressed batches
    • + *
+ * + * @see DefaultRecordBatch for the standard implementation + * @see MemoryRecords for in-memory batch collection */ public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable { @@ -65,10 +86,15 @@ public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable { /** * Check whether the checksum of this batch is correct. * - * @return true If so, false otherwise + * @return {@code true} if the checksum is valid, {@code false} otherwise + * @see #checksum() */ boolean isValid(); /** * Raise an exception if the checksum is not valid. + * + * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.CorruptRecordException if the stored checksum + * does not match the computed checksum */ void ensureValid(); @@ -92,9 +118,16 @@ public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable { /** * Get the timestamp type of this record batch. This will be {@link TimestampType#NO_TIMESTAMP_TYPE} * if the batch has magic 0. * + *

Timestamp type is stored in the batch attributes and indicates whether timestamps represent + * the time when the record was produced (CREATE_TIME) or the time it was appended to the broker + * log (LOG_APPEND_TIME). + * * @return The timestamp type + * @see #maxTimestamp() + * @see TimestampType */ TimestampType timestampType(); /** * Get the base offset contained in this record batch. For magic version prior to 2, the base offset will * always be the offset of the first message in the batch. This generally requires deep iteration and will @@ -110,6 +143,9 @@ public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable { * * @return The base offset of this record batch (which may or may not be the offset of the first record * as described above). + * @implNote For magic v0-v1, this method performs deep iteration which has O(n) complexity. + * For magic v2+, this is O(1) and reads from the batch header. + * @see #lastOffset() */ long baseOffset(); @@ -145,17 +181,39 @@ public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable { /** * Get the producer id for this log record batch. For older magic versions, this will return -1. * + *

The producer ID is used to identify idempotent and transactional producers. Non-idempotent + * producers will have a producer ID of {@link #NO_PRODUCER_ID} (-1). + * * @return The producer id or -1 if there is none + * @see #hasProducerId() + * @see #producerEpoch() */ long producerId(); /** * Get the producer epoch for this log record batch. * - * @return The producer epoch, or -1 if there is none + *

The producer epoch represents the incarnation of the producer ID and is used to detect + * stale idempotent producer requests. For non-idempotent producers, this value is {@link #NO_PRODUCER_EPOCH} (-1). + * + * @return The producer epoch, or -1 if there is none (for non-idempotent producers) + * @see #producerId() */ short producerEpoch(); /** * Does the batch have a valid producer id set. + * + *

This is a convenience method equivalent to checking if {@code producerId() != NO_PRODUCER_ID}. + * Valid producer IDs are >= 0. + * + * @return {@code true} if this batch has a valid producer ID (indicating idempotent or transactional + * production), {@code false} if the producer ID is {@link #NO_PRODUCER_ID} + * @see #producerId() */ boolean hasProducerId(); @@ -177,7 +235,11 @@ public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable { /** * Get the compression type of this record batch. * * @return The compression type + * @see CompressionType + * @see #isCompressed() + * @see #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier) */ CompressionType compressionType(); @@ -204,10 +266,28 @@ public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable { /** * Write this record batch into a buffer. * @param buffer The buffer to write the batch to + * @throws java.nio.BufferOverflowException if the buffer does not have sufficient space + * @see #sizeInBytes() */ void writeTo(ByteBuffer buffer); /** * Whether or not this record batch is part of a transaction. - * @return true if it is, false otherwise + * + *

Transactional batches are part of an atomic multi-partition transaction and contain + * explicit commit/abort markers. They require broker-side enforcement of atomicity. + * + * @return {@code true} if this batch is transactional, {@code false} otherwise + * @see #isControlBatch() */ boolean isTransactional(); /** * Get the delete horizon, returns OptionalLong.EMPTY if the first timestamp is not the delete horizon + * + *

The delete horizon is used by log compaction to mark the timestamp up to which records + * with the same key should be deleted. It is typically set when a batch contains tombstones + * (records with null values) or transaction end markers. + * * @return timestamp of the delete horizon + * @see MemoryRecords#filterTo(TopicPartition, RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, int, BufferSupplier) */ OptionalLong deleteHorizonMs(); @@ -227,8 +307,20 @@ public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable { * Whether or not this record batch is part of a transaction. * Return a streaming iterator which basically delays decompression of the record stream until the records * are actually asked for using {@link Iterator#next()}. If the message format does not support streaming * iteration, then the normal iterator is returned. Either way, callers should ensure that the iterator is closed. * + *

Performance Note: This method is crucial for memory-efficient processing of large + * compressed batches. Instead of decompressing the entire batch at once, records are decompressed on-demand + * as the iterator is consumed. This requires careful buffer management via the supplied {@code decompressionBufferSupplier}. + * + *

For optimal performance with compressed batches: + *

    + *
  • Provide a {@link BufferSupplier} that reuses buffers across multiple batches
    • + *
  • Allocating a fresh large buffer (64 KB for LZ4) for each batch will dominate decompression cost
    • + *
  • Always close the returned iterator to release decompression resources
    • + *
* @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported. - * For small record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4) - * will dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the - * batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant - * performance impact. * @return The closeable iterator + * @see BufferSupplier + * @see CloseableIterator + * @see #iterator() for full decompression variant */ CloseableIterator streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier); /** * Check whether this is a control batch (i.e. whether the control bit is set in the batch attributes). * For magic versions prior to 2, this is always false. * + *

Control batches contain control records such as transaction abort/commit markers or + * leader epoch changes. These are system records, not user data, and are typically filtered + * out during consumption. + * * @return Whether this is a batch containing control records + * @see #isTransactional() + * @see ControlRecordType */ boolean isControlBatch(); } ``` --- ## 2. DefaultRecordBatch Implementation Enhancement ### File: `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/DefaultRecordBatch.java` ```diff --- a/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/DefaultRecordBatch.java +++ b/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/DefaultRecordBatch.java @@ -44,13 +44,72 @@ import static org.apache.kafka.common.record.Records.LOG_OVERHEAD; /** * RecordBatch implementation for magic 2 and above. The schema is given below: * - * RecordBatch =>\n * BaseOffset => Int64\n * Length => Int32\n * PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32\n * Magic => Int8\n * CRC => Uint32\n * Attributes => Int16\n * LastOffsetDelta => Int32 // also serves as LastSequenceDelta\n * FirstTimestamp => Int64\n * MaxTimestamp => Int64\n * ProducerId => Int64\n * ProducerEpoch => Int16\n * BaseSequence => Int32\n * Records => [Record]\n * * Note that when compression is enabled (see attributes below), the compressed record data is serialized * directly following the count of the number of records. * * The CRC covers the data from the attributes to the end of the batch (i.e. all the bytes that follow the CRC). It is * located after the magic byte, which means that clients must parse the magic byte before deciding how to interpret * the bytes between the batch length and the magic byte. The partition leader epoch field is not included in the CRC * computation to avoid the need to recompute the CRC when this field is assigned for every batch that is received by * the broker. The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used for the computation. * * On Compaction: Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 and above preserves the first and last offset/sequence * numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the * producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, then following * a partition leader failure, once the new leader has rebuilt the producer state from the log, the next sequence * expected number would no longer be in sync with what was written by the client. This would cause an * unexpected OutOfOrderSequence error, which is typically fatal. The base sequence number must be preserved for * duplicate checking: the broker checks incoming Produce requests for duplicates by verifying that the first and * last sequence numbers of the incoming batch match the last from that producer. * * Note that if all of the records in a batch are removed during compaction, the broker may still retain an empty * batch header in order to preserve the producer sequence information as described above. These empty batches * are retained only until either a new sequence number is written by the corresponding producer or the producerId * is expired from lack of activity. * * There is no similar need to preserve the timestamp from the original batch after compaction. The FirstTimestamp * field therefore always reflects the timestamp of the first record in the batch. If the batch is empty, the * FirstTimestamp will be set to -1 (NO_TIMESTAMP). * * Similarly, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the maximum timestamp of the current records if the timestamp type * is CREATE_TIME. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, on the other hand, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the timestamp set * by the broker and is preserved after compaction. Additionally, the MaxTimestamp of an empty batch always retains * the previous value prior to becoming empty. * * The delete horizon flag for the sixth bit is used to determine if the first timestamp of the batch had been set to * the time for which tombstones / transaction markers need to be removed. If it is true, then the first timestamp is * the delete horizon, otherwise, it is merely the first timestamp of the record batch. * * The current attributes are given below: * * --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- * | Unused (7-15) | Delete Horizon Flag (6) | Control (5) | Transactional (4) | Timestamp Type (3) | Compression Type (0-2) | * --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- + * + *

Thread Safety

+ * Not thread-safe. This class wraps a {@link java.nio.ByteBuffer} which is not thread-safe. + * The underlying buffer is typically read-only after construction, but the class does support mutable operations + * (e.g., {@link #setMaxTimestamp(TimestampType, long)}, {@link #setPartitionLeaderEpoch(int)}) which can be used + * during log compaction. Any concurrent access requires external synchronization. + * + *

Performance Characteristics

+ *
    + *
  • Header access (O(1)): All metadata fields (offset, timestamp, producer ID, CRC) are accessed + * directly from the underlying buffer with constant time complexity
    • + *
  • Record iteration: Uncompressed batches iterate in O(n) time. Compressed batches require + * decompression; use {@link #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)} for streaming decompression to avoid loading + * the entire batch into memory
    • + *
  • Lazy iteration: The {@link #iterator()} method fully decompresses compressed batches upfront + * for convenience, while {@link #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)} delays decompression until records are consumed
    • + *
  • CRC computation: The CRC is lazily computed only when validation is needed
    • + *
+ * + * @see RecordBatch for the interface contract + * @see MemoryRecords for container of multiple batches + * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder for building new batches */ public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRecordBatch { static final int BASE_OFFSET_OFFSET = 0; @@ -164,7 +223,17 @@ public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRe /** * Gets the base timestamp of the batch which is used to calculate the record timestamps from the deltas. * + *

All record timestamps within this batch are stored as deltas from this base timestamp. + * The actual record timestamp is computed as {@code baseTimestamp + timestampDelta}. + * This encoding significantly reduces storage space for batches with many records. + * + *

In a LOG_APPEND_TIME batch, the base timestamp is set to the time the broker appended the batch. + * In a CREATE_TIME batch, it is the timestamp of the first record (or -1 if the batch becomes empty during compaction). + * * @return The base timestamp + * @see #maxTimestamp() + * @see #timestampType() + * @see DefaultRecord for individual record timestamp computation */ public long baseTimestamp() { return buffer.getLong(BASE_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET); @@ -276,13 +345,26 @@ public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRe + /** + * Get a DataInputStream for reading records from this batch. + * + *

This method returns an input stream positioned at the start of the records section of the batch. + * If compression is enabled, the stream will decompress records on-the-fly. The caller is responsible + * for closing the returned stream to release any decompression resources. + * + * @param bufferSupplier Supplier for allocation of decompression buffers, if needed + * @return A DataInputStream ready to read records; caller must close this stream + * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.KafkaException if decompression fails + * @see #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier) + * @see #iterator() + */ public DataInputStream recordInputStream(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) { final ByteBuffer buffer = this.buffer.duplicate(); buffer.position(RECORDS_OFFSET); return new DataInputStream(compressionType().wrapForInput(buffer, magic(), bufferSupplier)); } + /** + * Creates a closeable iterator for compressed records with optional key/value skipping. + * + * @param bufferSupplier Supplier for decompression buffers + * @param skipKeyValue If true, only reads record metadata and skips key, value, and headers + * @return A CloseableIterator that must be closed by the caller + */ private CloseableIterator compressedIterator(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier, boolean skipKeyValue) { final DataInputStream inputStream = recordInputStream(bufferSupplier); @@ -303,6 +385,11 @@ public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRe } } + /** + * Creates a closeable iterator for uncompressed records. + * + * @return A CloseableIterator for uncompressed records in this batch + */ private CloseableIterator uncompressedIterator() { final ByteBuffer buffer = this.buffer.duplicate(); buffer.position(RECORDS_OFFSET); @@ -345,11 +432,33 @@ public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRe + /** + * Creates an iterator that skips key/value/header data for memory efficiency. + * + *

This method is used during log validation to check record count and offsets without loading + * full record data into memory. For uncompressed batches, this provides minimal savings since + * the ByteBufferInputStream's skip() operation is not significantly more efficient than reading. + * For compressed batches, the savings are substantial. + * + * @param bufferSupplier Supplier for decompression buffers; must be closed by caller + * @return A CloseableIterator that yields records with null key/value/headers + * @see #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier) for full record iteration with compression support + */ @Override public CloseableIterator skipKeyValueIterator(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) { if (count() == 0) { return CloseableIterator.wrap(Collections.emptyIterator()); @@ -373,6 +482,16 @@ public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRe + /** + * Set the last offset in this batch. + * + *

This is used during log compaction to update the last offset when records are filtered out. + * The base offset is adjusted to maintain the offset delta. This method updates the buffer in-place + * without recomputing the CRC. + * + * @param offset The new last offset (must be >= baseOffset) + */ @Override public void setLastOffset(long offset) { buffer.putLong(BASE_OFFSET_OFFSET, offset - lastOffsetDelta()); @@ -380,6 +499,23 @@ public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRe + /** + * Set the maximum timestamp and timestamp type of this batch. + * + *

This is used during log compaction and deletion to update timestamp metadata. If both the timestamp type + * and value are unchanged, this method returns immediately without modifying the buffer or recomputing the CRC. + * Otherwise, the attributes byte is updated, the max timestamp is written, and the CRC is recomputed to cover + * the modified data. + * + *

Performance Note: CRC recomputation is expensive. This method should be called judiciously + * during log compaction. Batching multiple updates is not supported; each call triggers a full CRC recomputation. + * + * @param timestampType The new timestamp type (CREATE_TIME or LOG_APPEND_TIME) + * @param maxTimestamp The new maximum timestamp + * @see #checksum() + * @see #setPartitionLeaderEpoch(int) + */ @Override public void setMaxTimestamp(TimestampType timestampType, long maxTimestamp) { long currentMaxTimestamp = maxTimestamp(); @@ -394,6 +530,13 @@ public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRe } + /** + * Set the partition leader epoch for this batch. + * + *

The partition leader epoch is recorded but excluded from CRC computation to avoid recomputation + * when the broker assigns the epoch upon receiving the batch. + * + * @param epoch The partition leader epoch + */ @Override public void setPartitionLeaderEpoch(int epoch) { buffer.putInt(PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH_OFFSET, epoch); @@ -448,13 +591,48 @@ public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRe + /** + * Write an empty record batch header to the buffer. + * + *

Empty batches are written during log compaction when all records in a batch are removed but the + * batch header must be retained to preserve producer sequence information. This allows the broker to + * maintain idempotent producer state and detect duplicate requests. + * + * @param buffer The buffer to write to + * @param magic The record format magic version + * @param producerId The producer ID + * @param producerEpoch The producer epoch + * @param baseSequence The base sequence number + * @param baseOffset The base offset + * @param lastOffset The last offset (used to compute offset delta) + * @param partitionLeaderEpoch The partition leader epoch + * @param timestampType The timestamp type (CREATE_TIME or LOG_APPEND_TIME) + * @param timestamp The timestamp value + * @param isTransactional Whether this is a transactional batch + * @param isControlRecord Whether this is a control batch + */ public static void writeEmptyHeader(ByteBuffer buffer, byte magic, long producerId, @@ -466,11 +644,87 @@ public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRe + /** + * Write a complete record batch header to the buffer. + * + *

This is the primary method for serializing batch metadata into a ByteBuffer. It validates input + * parameters, computes the attributes byte from component flags, writes all header fields in the correct + * binary layout, and computes the CRC over the attributes and all subsequent data. + * + *

Layout: This method assumes the buffer is positioned at the batch start and writes + * all header fields starting from BASE_OFFSET_OFFSET. After writing, the buffer position is advanced to + * RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD, ready to write record data. + * + *

Thread Safety: This static method modifies the provided buffer in-place. The caller must + * ensure exclusive access to the buffer during the write operation. + * + *

CRC Computation: The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) algorithm is used, covering bytes from + * ATTRIBUTES_OFFSET to the end of the provided size range. The partition leader epoch field is intentionally + * excluded from CRC computation. + * * @param buffer The buffer to write to (must have sufficient remaining space for batch header + records) * @param baseOffset The base offset of the batch * @param lastOffsetDelta The last offset relative to base offset * @param sizeInBytes Total size of the batch including the LOG_OVERHEAD header * @param magic The record format version (must be >= CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE) * @param compressionType The compression algorithm used for records * @param timestampType The timestamp type (CREATE_TIME or LOG_APPEND_TIME; must not be NO_TIMESTAMP_TYPE) * @param firstTimestamp The timestamp of the first record (or NO_TIMESTAMP if batch is empty) * @param maxTimestamp The maximum timestamp in the batch * @param producerId The producer ID (or NO_PRODUCER_ID for non-idempotent producers) * @param epoch The producer epoch (or NO_PRODUCER_EPOCH for non-idempotent producers) * @param sequence The base sequence number (or NO_SEQUENCE for non-idempotent producers) * @param isTransactional Whether this batch is part of a transaction * @param isControlBatch Whether this batch contains control records * @param isDeleteHorizonSet Whether the delete horizon flag is set * @param partitionLeaderEpoch The partition leader epoch assigned by the broker * @param numRecords The number of records in the batch * + * @throws IllegalArgumentException if magic < CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE or firstTimestamp is invalid + * @see #RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD for the size of the header in bytes + * @see Crc32C for CRC computation details */ public static void writeHeader(ByteBuffer buffer, long baseOffset, ``` --- ## 3. MemoryRecords Implementation Enhancement ### File: `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/MemoryRecords.java` ```diff --- a/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/MemoryRecords.java +++ b/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/MemoryRecords.java @@ -44,9 +44,48 @@ import java.util.Objects; import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.Utils; /** - * A {@link Records} implementation backed by a ByteBuffer. This is used only for reading or - * modifying in-place an existing buffer of record batches. To create a new buffer see {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder}, - * or one of the {@link #builder(ByteBuffer, byte, CompressionType, TimestampType, long)} variants. + * A {@link Records} implementation backed by a ByteBuffer. + * + *

Purpose

+ * MemoryRecords provides an in-memory representation of Kafka record batches, supporting reading, iteration, + * filtering, and format downconversion. It is the primary interface for batch-level operations on data in memory + * (as opposed to {@link FileRecords} for file-backed storage or {@link UnalignedMemoryRecords} for partial buffers). + * + *

Usage

+ *
    + *
  • Reading batches: Use {@link #readableRecords(ByteBuffer)} to wrap an existing ByteBuffer + * containing serialized batches, then iterate with {@link #batches()}
    • + *
  • Creating new records: Use static factory methods like {@link #builder(ByteBuffer, CompressionType, TimestampType, long)} + * or {@link #withRecords(byte, CompressionType, SimpleRecord...)} to build new batches
    • + *
  • Filtering/compaction: Use {@link #filterTo(TopicPartition, RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, int, BufferSupplier)} + * to apply retention policies and deduplicate records during log compaction
    • + *
  • Format conversion: Use {@link #downConvert(byte, long, Time)} to convert between magic versions
    • + *
+ * + *

Thread Safety

+ * Not thread-safe. The underlying {@link java.nio.ByteBuffer} is not thread-safe. Additionally, + * iteration state (via {@link #batches()}) is not thread-safe. Read-only access (e.g., multiple threads reading + * from a shared buffer) requires external synchronization if the buffer contents are modified. Most use cases + * involve single-threaded access during log reading, network transmission, or batch processing. + * + *

Performance Characteristics

+ *
    + *
  • Lazy batch parsing (O(n) on first use): Batches are parsed on-demand during iteration. + * The {@link #validBytes()} computation iterates all batches to exclude partial, trailing data
    • + *
  • Filtering (O(m) with decompression cost): {@link #filterTo(TopicPartition, RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, int, BufferSupplier)} + * may require decompressing compressed batches if records are filtered. Use a buffer-reusing + * {@link BufferSupplier} for optimal performance
    • + *
  • Buffer write cost: {@link #writeFullyTo(GatheringByteChannel)} may allocate internal + * buffers if writes are partial; use in a loop until all bytes are written
    • + *
+ * + *

Memory Management

+ * MemoryRecords does not take ownership of the backing ByteBuffer. Callers are responsible for managing + * the buffer's lifecycle. Buffer duplicates created during iteration do not share position/limit state with the original. + * + * @see RecordBatch for the batch interface + * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder for building new MemoryRecords + * @see FileRecords for file-backed storage */ public class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords { private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(MemoryRecords.class); @@ -67,7 +106,15 @@ public class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords { @Override public int sizeInBytes() { return buffer.limit(); } @Override public long writeTo(TransferableChannel channel, long position, int length) throws IOException { + /** + * Write a range of records to a network channel. + * + * @param channel The channel to write to + * @param position The starting byte position in the buffer + * @param length The number of bytes to write + * @return The number of bytes written (may be less than requested) + * @throws IOException If a write error occurs + * @throws IllegalArgumentException if position > Integer.MAX_VALUE or position + length > buffer.limit() + */ if (position > Integer.MAX_VALUE) throw new IllegalArgumentException("position should not be greater than Integer.MAX_VALUE: " + position); if (position + length > buffer.limit()) @@ -88,10 +135,22 @@ public class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords { /** * Write all records to the given channel (including partial records). + * + *

This method repeatedly calls {@link GatheringByteChannel#write(ByteBuffer)} until the entire + * buffer is written. It is useful for network serialization when the buffer may not be fully written + * in a single system call. + * * @param channel The channel to write to * @return The number of bytes written * @throws IOException For any IO errors writing to the channel + * @see #writeTo(TransferableChannel, long, int) for position-aware writes */ public int writeFullyTo(GatheringByteChannel channel) throws IOException { buffer.mark(); @@ -114,6 +173,19 @@ public class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords { + /** + * Returns the number of valid bytes in the buffer, excluding any partial trailing batches. + * + *

The {@link #sizeInBytes()} method returns the full buffer limit, while this method only counts + * complete batches. A batch is considered valid if it contains a complete header and does not exceed the buffer. + * This distinction is important for log reading where the final batch may be incomplete (e.g., received + * partially over the network). + * + *

Performance Note: On first invocation, this method iterates all batches to compute + * the total, then caches the result. Subsequent calls return the cached value. + * * @return The number of valid bytes + * @see #sizeInBytes() */ public int validBytes() { if (validBytes >= 0) @@ -138,25 +206,56 @@ public class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords { + /** + * Parse the header of the first batch in the buffer. + * + *

This method validates that the buffer contains sufficient data for a complete batch header + * (up to and including the magic byte) and determines the total size of the first batch including + * the 12-byte LOG_OVERHEAD header. + * * @return first batch size including LOG_OVERHEAD if buffer contains header up to * magic byte, null otherwise * @throws CorruptRecordException if record size or magic is invalid + * @see Records#HEADER_SIZE_UP_TO_MAGIC + * @see Records#LOG_OVERHEAD */ public Integer firstBatchSize() { if (buffer.remaining() < HEADER_SIZE_UP_TO_MAGIC) return null; return new ByteBufferLogInputStream(buffer, Integer.MAX_VALUE).nextBatchSize(); } /** * Filter the records into the provided ByteBuffer. * + *

This method applies a {@link RecordFilter} to selectively include or exclude records and batches, + * producing filtered output in the destination buffer. It handles compression transparently by + * decompressing batches that need per-record filtering, then re-serializing the filtered records. + * For batches that are entirely retained or deleted, the original serialized form is preserved + * when possible to avoid recompression overhead. + * + *

Compaction Semantics: This method implements log compaction's delete horizon logic: + * tombstone batches (those containing only null-valued records) trigger a delete horizon to be set, + * which marks timestamps for cleanup. Empty batches are retained to preserve producer sequence state. + * + *

Memory Management: If the filtered records exceed the destination buffer capacity, + * a new larger buffer is allocated and returned in the {@link FilterResult#outputBuffer()}. Callers must + * check for this and use the returned buffer instead. + * * @param partition The partition that is filtered (used only for logging) * @param filter The filter function * @param destinationBuffer The byte buffer to write the filtered records to * @param maxRecordBatchSize The maximum record batch size. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records - * regardless of compression. + * A record batch is a container for records in the Kafka protocol. + * + * In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1), a batch consisted of either a single record + * (if no compression was enabled) or multiple records (if compression was used). Newer versions (magic + * versions 2 and above) generally contain many records regardless of compression strategy. + * + *

Role in Kafka Protocol

+ * RecordBatch is the fundamental unit of data organization in Kafka logs and network communication. Each + * batch contains metadata (offsets, timestamps, producer information, CRC checksum) and a sequence of + * individual records. The batch format supports idempotent producers, transactional writes, and compression. + * + *
